title: LLMs and Generative AI in the Real World

learning_objectives: Understand what LLMs do and where they can fail.

lesson.title: LLM Use Cases and Failure Modes

blocks.title: Week 6 Focus
blocks.content: Students compare AI outputs and learn to verify generated content.

items.label: LLM Tasks
items.description: Use summarization, drafting, extraction, and classification examples.

items.label: Verification
items.description: Check outputs instead of trusting them automatically.

items.label: Summarize
items.description: Condense large text into a shorter explanation.

items.label: Draft
items.description: Create a first version of an email or report.

items.label: Extract
items.description: Pull dates, names, or themes from content.

items.label: Classify
items.description: Sort text into labels such as positive or negative.

blocks.title: Responsible AI Reminder
blocks.content: Generated text can look polished while still containing factual mistakes.

activity.title: Output Comparison Lab
activity.instructions: Compare two AI answers and explain which one is more trustworthy.
activity.success_criteria: Explain one strength and one risk in the selected answer.

questions.prompt: Which task is a common use case for an LLM?
questions.explanation: Summarization is a typical text task for large language models.

questions.prompt: What is hallucination in generative AI?
options: A confident but false or unsupported answer
questions.explanation: Hallucination describes generated content that sounds plausible but is wrong or unsupported.

questions.prompt: What is a good habit after getting an AI answer?
questions.explanation: Important claims should be checked against trusted sources or human review.

reflection.prompt: Describe one useful way to use an LLM and one reason the answer still needs human review.
reflection.placeholder: LLMs can summarize a long article, but I should still verify facts because the summary may miss or invent details.

teacher_notes: Run the same prompt in more than one AI tool to show differences in style and reliability.
Always fact-check at least one answer live in class.
